questionText: Consider the two statements $P:\ x = 3$ and $Q:\ x^2 = 9$, where $x$ is real. State, with full justification, which one of the symbols $\Rightarrow$, $\Leftarrow$ or $\Leftrightarrow$ correctly connects $P$ and $Q$. Hence say whether $P$ is a sufficient condition, a necessary condition, both or neither, for $Q$.

steps.description: Write down the two statements to be compared.
steps.explanation: Naming the statements $P$ and $Q$ lets us discuss direction without rewriting them each time. The whole task is about which statement forces the other, so we keep both clearly in view.

steps.description: Recall what each connecting symbol claims.
steps.workingLatex: P \Rightarrow Q\ (P \text{ forces } Q),\quad P \Leftarrow Q\ (Q \text{ forces } P),\quad P \Leftrightarrow Q\ (\text{both})
steps.explanation: Choosing a symbol is really choosing a direction of implication. Because $\Leftrightarrow$ needs both one-way arrows to hold, we must test each direction separately.

steps.description: Test the forward direction P implies Q.
steps.explanation: We assume $P$ and substitute into $x^2$. Squaring $3$ gives exactly $9$, so whenever $x=3$ the statement $Q$ is guaranteed.

steps.description: Conclude the forward implication is true.
steps.explanation: The substitution left no room for doubt, since the value $x=3$ produces $x^2=9$ every time. So $P$ on its own is enough to secure $Q$.

steps.description: Test the reverse direction Q implies P.
steps.explanation: Now we assume $Q$ and ask whether $x$ must equal $3$. Solving $x^2=9$ gives two roots, so knowing the square is $9$ does not pin down the sign of $x$.

steps.description: Produce a counterexample to the reverse direction.
steps.explanation: The value $x=-3$ satisfies $Q$ yet fails $P$, and one counterexample is enough to break an implication. This shows $Q$ does not force $P$.
Only the forward arrow survives testing, so the one-way symbol from $P$ to $Q$ is correct. The failure of the reverse direction rules out $\Leftrightarrow$.

steps.description: Translate into necessary and sufficient language.
steps.workingLatex: P \text{ is sufficient for } Q, \text{ but not necessary}
steps.explanation: Because $P$ guarantees $Q$, $P$ is sufficient, meaning enough. Because $Q$ can hold via $x=-3$ without $P$, $P$ is not necessary, meaning not required.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: $x = 3 \Rightarrow x^2 = 9$ only. So $x = 3$ is a sufficient but not a necessary condition for $x^2 = 9$.
